elements.sent-2411-001: Gaining insights from realistic dynamical models of biochemical systems can be challenging given their large number of state variables.
elements.sent-2411-002: Model reduction techniques can mitigate this by decreasing complexity by mapping the model onto a lower-dimensional state space.
elements.sent-2411-003: Exact constrained lumping identifies reductions as linear combinations of the original state variables in systems of nonlinear ordinary differential equations, preserving specific user-defined output variables without error.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, exact reductions can be too stringent in practice, as model parameters are often uncertain or imprecise---a particularly relevant problem for biochemical systems.
elements.sent-2411-006: It allows for a relaxation of exactness within a given tolerance parameter $\varepsilon$, while still working in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-007: We prove that the accuracy, i.e., the difference between the output variables in the original and reduced model, is in the order of $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we provide a heuristic algorithm to find the smallest $\varepsilon$ for a given maximum allowable size of the lumped system.
elements.sent-2411-009: Our method is applied to several models from the literature, resulting in coarser aggregations than exact lumping while still capturing the dynamics of the original system accurately.
elements.sent-2411-010: Biochemical models based on dynamical systems help discover mechanistic principles in living organisms and predict their behavior under unforeseen circumstances.
elements.sent-2411-011: Realistic and accurate models, however, often require considerable detail that inevitably leads to large state spaces.
elements.sent-2411-012: This hinders both human intelligibility and numerical/computational analysis.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, even the relatively primary mechanism of protein phosphorylation may yield a state space that grows combinatorial with the number of phosphorylation sites~\cite{FEBS:FEBS7027 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: As a general way to cope with large state spaces, model reduction aims to provide a lower-dimensional representation of the system under study that retains some dynamic properties of interest to the modeler.
elements.sent-2411-015: In applications to computational systems biology, it is beneficial that the reduced state space keeps physical interpretability, mainly when the model is used to validate mechanistic hypotheses~\cite{10.1093/bioinformatics/btn035,21899762,Apri201216 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: There is a variety of approaches in this context, such as those exploiting time-scale separation properties~\cite{okino1998,DBLP:journals/tac/WhitbyCKLTT22 , quasi-steady-state approximation~\cite{SegelS89,radulescu2012reduction , heuristic fitness functions~\cite{10.1145/3071178.3071265 , spatial regularity~\cite{DBLP:journals/pe/TschaikowskiT17 , sensitivity analysis~\cite{Snowden:2017aa , and conservation analysis, which detects linear combinations of variables that are constant at all times~\cite{10.1093/bioinformatics/bti800 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: By lumping one generally refers to a reduction method that provides a self-consistent system of dynamical equations comprised of a set of macro-variables, each given as a combination of the original ones~\cite{aokiOptimizationStochasticSystems1967, kemenyFiniteMarkovChains1960,okino1998,Snowden:2017aa .}
elements.sent-2411-018: In linear lumping, this reduction is expressed as a linear transformation of the original state variables.
elements.sent-2411-019: Since this can destroy physical intelligibility in general, constrained lumping allows restricting to only part of the state space by defining linear combinations of state variables that ought to be preserved in the reduction~\cite{LI199195 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Lumping techniques of Markov chains date back to the early nineties~\cite{Larsen19911 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: These were later extended to stochastic process algebra~\cite{10.1007/978-0-387-09680-3_18,10.1007/978-3-540-88479-8_13,10.1093/comjnl/bxr094,DBLP:conf/splc/Tribastone14,TSCHAIKOWSKI2014140 , and have been expanded more recently to efficient algorithmic approaches for stochastic chemical reaction networks~\cite{DBLP:journals/bioinformatics/CardelliPTTVW21 and deterministic models of biological systems based on ordinary differential equations (ODEs) with polynomial right-hand sides~\cite{CARDELLI2019132,pnas17,DBLP:conf/lics/CardelliTTV16 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In these cases, the reduction is a specific kind of linear mapping induced by a partition (i.e., an equivalence relation) of state variables; in the aggregated system, each macro-variable represents the sum of the original variables of a partition block.
elements.sent-2411-023: For polynomial ODE systems, CLUE has been presented as an algorithm that computes constrained linear lumping efficiently, i.e., in polynomial time~\cite{ovchinnikov_clue_2021 , by avoiding the symbolic computation of the eigenvalues of a non-constant matrix that would have required in prior approaches~\cite{LiRabitz,LI199195 .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In particular, CLUE can compute the smallest linear dimensional reduction that preserves the dynamics of arbitrary linear combinations of original state variables given by the user.
elements.sent-2411-025: A complete step-by-step example of CLUE reduction on a synthetic 3-variables model is provided later (Exampe~\ref{ex:firstode ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: To give a concrete example of the usefulness of CLUE applied to a real model, we consider a model of FceRI-like network of a cell-surface receptor~\cite{borisov_domain-oriented_2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The observable or quantity of interest is the amount of Phosphorylation at site Y2 (S2P), which is the sum of 10 out of the 24 variables in the model.

elements.sent-2411-031: Approximate lumping is a natural extension that has been studied for a long time (e.g.,~\cite{LI1990977 ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: Indeed, although exact reduction methods have been experimentally proved successful in a large variety of biological systems (e.g.,~\cite{SBML ), approximate reductions can be more robust to parametric uncertainty---which notoriously affects systems biology models (e.g.,~\cite{doi:10.1098/rsif.2017.0237,DBLP:conf/cmsb/BarnatBBDHPS17 )---and offer a flexible trade-off between the aggressiveness of the reduction and its precision.}}

elements.algorithm-2411-004: \caption{Approximate Constrained Lumping Algorithm}\label{alg:acl} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE numerical threshold $\varepsilon \geq 0$, and a set of matrices $\left\{ J_1,\dots,J_N \right\}$ spanning $\mathcal{V}_{J}$ (Theorem \ref{thm:repJ}), and a $p \times m$ matrix of observables $M$ with row rank $p$ \STATE compute orthonormal rows spanning the row space of $M$ and store them as $M$ \STATE set $L := M$\label{alg:acl:setL0} \REPEAT \FORALL{$1 \leq i \leq N$ and rows $r$ of $L$} \label{alg:acl:mainloop} \STATE {compute $\pi_{i} := r J_i L^T L$} \label{alg:acl:proj} \IF{$\norm{r J_i - \pi_{i}} > \varepsilon$}\label{alg:acl:check} \STATE {append row $(r J_i - \pi_{i}) / \norm{r J_i -\pi_{i} }$ to $L$}\label{alg:acl:append} \ENDIF \ENDFOR \UNTIL{no rows have been appended to $L$} \RETURN lumping matrix $L$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-182: In Line \ref{alg:acl:append of Algorithm \ref{alg:acl we do not append $rJ_{i}$, given by the orange vector in Figure \ref{fig:newrow .}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: Rather, we append the normalized component of $rJ_i$ in the orthogonal direction to $\rsp(L)$, which is $r J_i - \pi_{i}$, the green vector in Fig.~\ref{fig:newrow .}
elements.sent-2411-184: This ensures that the matrix $L$ is orthonormal.
elements.sent-2411-185: We can use the fact that the pseudoinverse of an orthonormal matrix is its transpose to obtain that $\bar{L =L^{T}$.}
elements.sent-2411-186: We now provide a detailed example of Algorithm~\ref{alg:acl .}

elements.sent-2411-215: For this reason, we introduce a heuristic approach to appropriately choose $\varepsilon$.
elements.sent-2411-216: Intuitively, by increasing $\varepsilon$, it is possible to lump more variables together at the price of larger approximation errors.
elements.sent-2411-217: We would like to find the largest admissible $\varepsilon$ such that the approximation error is small enough.
elements.sent-2411-218: To this aim, we begin by noting that the minimum value that $\varepsilon$ can have is $0$, corresponding to an exact reduction.

elements.sent-2411-220: However, this requires an appropriate choice of increment which in turn depends on the model.
elements.sent-2411-221: Instead, Lemma \ref{lmm:uppereps gives us an upper bound for $\varepsilon$ which can be computed for each model.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Let $\varepsilon_{\max}$ be given by \begin{equation}\label{eq:epsmax} \varepsilon_{\max} = \max_{j,i} \norm{r_{j}J_{i} - r_{j}J_{i}P_L},\end{equation} for $i=1,\dots, n$ and each $j=1,\dots, l$, where $P_L$ is the projection onto $\rsp(L)$.
elements.sent-2411-223: Then $\varepsilon_{max}$ is the smallest $\varepsilon$ such for any $\varepsilon \geq \varepsilon_{\max}$ the output of Algorithm \ref{alg:acl will be a matrix of orthonormal rows spanning the row space of $L$.}
elements.sent-2411-224: Lemma \ref{lmm:uppereps states that any $\varepsilon \geq \varepsilon_{\max}$ will collapse the dynamics of the system onto observable $M$.}
elements.sent-2411-225: In this way, for any given model, we have a range $[0;\varepsilon_{\max ]$ from which to choose the right value for $\varepsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-226: The value of $\varepsilon_{\max}$ depends not only on the chosen model and observable but also on the set of matrices $\mathcal{V _{J}$ used to represent $J(x)$.}

elements.sent-2411-237: Using the fact that the size of a reduced model decreases monotonically with $\varepsilon$, we set up a target (a cutoff size, $m^{*}$) for the reduced model given as a percentage of its original size.
elements.sent-2411-238: We then apply a binary search algorithm to find the largest $\varepsilon$ such that the reduced model's size $m_{\varepsilon}$ satisfies $m_{\varepsilon \leq m^{*}$.}
elements.sent-2411-239: In terms of Figure \ref{fig:staircase , we want to obtain the smallest $\varepsilon$ whose induced size is below the cutoff size.}
elements.sent-2411-240: This intuition is formalized by Algorithm \ref{alg:acleps .}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: \caption{Finding the smallest acceptable $\varepsilon$ for Algorithm \ref{alg:acl} } \begin{algorithmic}[1]\label{alg:acleps} \REQUIRE set of matrices $J_{i}, ~i=1,\dots,N$ spanning $\mathcal{V}_{J}$, and cutoff reduced size $m^{*}$, and minimal difference $d_{\min}$ \STATE {set $\varepsilon_{\min}=0$} \STATE {compute $m_{\min} = nrows(L_{\varepsilon_{\min}})$, $\varepsilon_{\max} = \max_{j,i} \norm{r_{j}J_{i}}$, $m_{\max} = nrows(L_{\varepsilon_{\max}})$} \IF{$m^{*}<m_{\min}$} \label{alg:acleps:check} \RETURN $\varepsilon_{\min}$ \ELSIF{$m^{*}>m_{\max}$} \RETURN $\varepsilon_{\max}$ \ENDIF \REPEAT \label{alg:acleps:mainloop} \STATE {compute $\varepsilon = (\varepsilon_{\max}+\varepsilon_{\min})/{2}$} \STATE {compute $L$ using Algorithm~\ref{alg:acl} with $\varepsilon$ } \IF{ $nrows(L) < m^{*} $ } \STATE {set $\varepsilon_{\max} = \varepsilon$ } \ELSE \STATE {set $\varepsilon_{\min} = \varepsilon$ } \ENDIF \STATE {compute $d= \varepsilon_{\max}-\varepsilon_{\min}$} \UNTIL{$d < d_{\min}$} \RETURN $\varepsilon_{\max}$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-254: In this section, we evaluate our method.
elements.sent-2411-255: All experiments were performed using an extension of the tool for CLUE~\cite{ovchinnikov_clue_2021,jimenez_clue_2022, cmsb2024_clue , an open-source Python library.}
elements.sent-2411-256: The experiments are available in the following repository.
elements.sent-2411-257: In Section~\ref{sec_eval:scal , we use a perturbed multisite protein phosphorylation model to show the scalability of our approach.}
elements.sent-2411-258: To evaluate the reduction power of our approach, we selected several models from the literature.
elements.sent-2411-259: These models are chemical reaction networks with $5$ or more species.
elements.sent-2411-260: We used two sources to select models.
elements.sent-2411-261: The first is a collection of polynomial models from the literature written in the input format of the tool BioNetGen~\cite{Blinov22112004 .}
elements.sent-2411-263: The second is the so-called ODEbase project~\cite{LuedersSturmRadulescu22 which offers importing/exporting capabilities for polynomial and rational models from the BiomodelsDB repository~\cite{BioModels2010 .}}
elements.sent-2411-265: Models with prefix BIOMD were taken from ODEbase, while the remaining models were taken directly from the supplementary material of their original paper.
elements.sent-2411-266: For each model, the observable was chosen according to the observables studied in the original paper describing the model.
elements.sent-2411-267: These typically consist of one variable or linear combinations of them.
elements.sent-2411-268: For models where more than one observable was studied in the original paper, we arbitrarily selected one of them.
elements.sent-2411-269: Models written in the BioNetGen format were first imported by ERODE~\cite{cardelli_erode_2017 , our tool that collects many techniques for the reduction of biological systems, and then exported in ERODE's .ode format supported by CLUE.}
elements.sent-2411-270: To import models from the ODEbase project~\cite{LuedersSturmRadulescu22 , instead, we directly implemented in CLUE an importer to convert them in .ode format.}
elements.sent-2411-271: When available, the set of initial conditions necessary for simulations was taken from the original paper or the model files in BiomodelsDB~\cite{BioModels2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-272: The time horizon was either taken from the original reference or chosen experimentally by noticing when steady state was reached.
elements.sent-2411-273: All this additional information was manually added to the .ode files to the corresponding model.
elements.sent-2411-274: When choosing the models, we aimed for a selection that could equally display our approach for both rational and polynomial models.
elements.sent-2411-275: The final selection of models is displayed in Table~\ref{tab:chosenmod .}
elements.sent-2411-276: The column Type reports whether the model is polynomial (P) or rational (R).
elements.sent-2411-277: The source paper presenting the model is displayed in the Ref. column.
elements.sent-2411-278: The column Description contains a summary of the biological system studied in the model.
elements.sent-2411-279: In column Observable, we can see the species (or linear combinations of them) chosen as a constraint for the lumping.
elements.sent-2411-280: Finally, column Size displays the number of model variables added in the observable over the total number of variables in the system (e.g., in Model 5, S2P sums 10 out of the 24 variables of the model).
